sections.text: Every word on this page was suggested by a visitor and applied by an AI agent through a real GitHub pull request. Suggest a change, watch the pipeline run, see the page update.

items: Moderation — Claude Haiku checks the suggestion for safety and relevance
Generation — Claude Sonnet produces a structured patch
Validation — Zod schemas enforce the patch shape before any commit
Commit — Octokit opens a pull request against the repository
Merge — CI runs the allowlist check; if it passes, GitHub auto-merges
Deploy — Vercel rebuilds; the page updates within 60 seconds

sections.body: A GitHub Actions workflow blocks any pull request that touches files outside a strict allowlist. The agent can only modify structured data — never application code, never component files. This means even a malicious or malformed suggestion cannot damage the site: the CI wall is enforced at the repository level, independent of the AI models entirely.
sections.title: The agent cannot break the site

sections.text: How the safety wall actually works

When the agent generates a patch and opens a pull request, a GitHub Actions CI workflow runs automatically. It inspects every file changed in the PR and checks each one against an allowlist of permitted paths: content/, theme/, and overrides/. If even a single file outside that allowlist is touched — intentionally or not — the workflow fails and the PR is blocked from merging. GitHub's branch protection rules enforce this: auto-merge cannot proceed until CI passes. The agent has no ability to bypass this check because the check runs on GitHub's infrastructure, not inside the agent pipeline.

items: AI moderation gate — Claude Haiku screens every suggestion before any Sonnet call is made, rejecting unsafe or off-topic content cheaply
Zod schema validation — the agent's output is validated against strict schemas before any commit happens; bad shapes or invalid values are caught here
Element locking — while a comment is in the pipeline, the same element cannot be targeted again, preventing race conditions
Kill switch — the owner can halt the entire pipeline instantly from the admin panel, before any API call is made
Rate limiting — 3 submissions per hour for anonymous users, 20 per hour for authenticated GitHub users, tracked by hashed IP
Anthropic spend cap — a hard monthly limit in the Anthropic console ensures financial exposure is fixed regardless of traffic

sections.body: The agent cannot invent new section types, write arbitrary HTML, or modify styling. It manipulates structured data that pre-built components know how to render. Constraints are the point.
sections.title: What the agent cannot do

sections.text: Structural edits mean the agent can split one paragraph into three, merge sections, reorder them, add a new callout, or remove a section entirely — not just swap words. The page shape itself is editable.

sections.code: agent(elevator-pitch): rewrite opening paragraph to active voice

items: "Add a section about the kill switch"
"Put the pipeline steps after the callout"
"Make the safety callout a success tone"

sections.text: The thing is interesting because it's real, not because it claims to be.

sections.attribution: docs/system-reference.md — on what this workflow actually does
sections.text: A suggestion can split a paragraph into three, reorder sections, add a callout, or remove a section entirely. A pure database cannot rearrange page structure. Every change has a real GitHub PR, real CI, and a real deploy — because real work is happening at each step.
Every change has a real pull request →
